FT_IC1.evidence: Study uses data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, baseline ages 9-10, U.S. ABCD cohort (N=10,574 early adolescents).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models examining cross-sectional and longitudinal associations between adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines and health outcomes at baseline and 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Journal of Adolescent Health (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds no phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient. Step 2 fails: the composite variable 'adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines' bundles sleep duration, screen time, and physical activity into a single multi-component guideline without separately reporting screen time as a standalone variable. The composite includes non-SM items (physical activity, sleep in the integrated framework) without breakout. Per binding rule: multi-device/multi-component screen time bundles fail unless per-device breakdown exists.

notes: Paper examines 24-hour integrated movement behaviors; screen time is measured but only as part of an adherence composite alongside sleep and physical activity. No isolated phone/SM variable reported.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study uses data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD), U.S. cohort (N=10,335 at year 2, N=6,095 at year 3).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable logistic regression examining cross-sectional (year 2) and longitudinal (year 2 to year 3) associations between cyberbullying victimization and substance use initiation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Drug and Alcohol Dependence (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: cyberbullying victimization is not a phone/SM-specific variable. It is a psychosocial exposure measured via self-report questionnaire on cyberbullying experience, not a quantifiable measure of phone or social media use (time on phone, time on SM, named platform use, texting, app use, etc.). Step 2: no composite of phone/SM items. Per binding clarification: 'Cyberbullying alone does not qualify' unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable with its own coefficient. This paper pairs cyberbullying alone with substance use; the IV is not phone/SM-specific.

notes: Paper examines cyberbullying victimization (measured via items: 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...') as the exposure, not phone/SM use. Cyberbullying is a form of harm that can occur online, but the variable itself is not a measure of phone or SM use quantity/frequency.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study uses data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, U.S. cohort (N=5,027 adolescents, ages 10-13 years baseline, assessed year 2 pre-pandemic and six time points May 2020-March 2021 during pandemic).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: mixed-effect models and generalized linear mixed models examining longitudinal changes in sleep and screen time before and during the COVID-19 pandemic, and associations between screen time and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Health Psychology (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 passes: paper reports phone/SM-specific estimates (variables labeled 'time spent on social media platforms' and 'time spent on single player video games' in minutes) paired with individual-trait-level sleep outcomes (time in bed, bedtime, sleep onset latency, number of awakenings). Multiple coefficients reported: social media use χ²(1)=114.56, p<.01 associated with shorter TIB; video games χ²(1)=6.13, p=.013 associated with TIB. These are quantifiable phone/SM variables paired with health/behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: regression coefficients, χ² test statistics, p-values, effect sizes, and unstandardized estimates throughout. E.g., 'time spent on social media platforms' χ²(1)=114.56, p<.01; 'time spent on single player video games' χ²(1)=6.13, p=.013; additional LMM model estimates for bedtime and sleep onset latency with p-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results for the SM↔sleep outcome relationship reported throughout main text and tables. Longitudinal and cross-sectional mixed-effects models with reported coefficients, χ² statistics, and interaction terms.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies: paper explicitly states 'Cross-sectional analyses were conducted for both T1 and T2 separately' examining associations between sleep and screen time. This cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis (sleep predicted by screen time) disqualifies FT-EC7 regardless of longitudinal analyses also present.
notes: Paper examines screen time (social media and video games) as IV and sleep outcomes as DV in both cross-sectional and longitudinal designs. Step 1 clearly met with named SM platforms and specific time measures.
